refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits during agreed network conditions.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting packet loss quality criteria. The refined FR isolates the quality/performance obligation (communication integrity) as the core functional requirement, removing the implicit 'support teleoperation' which is a higher-level system capability. The phrase 'communication quality meeting the customer-defined packet loss limit' is vague and prescriptive; 'maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits' is solution-neutral and measurable. The addition of 'during agreed network conditions' acknowledges the constraint context without embedding it as a design assumption.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation sessions across agreed network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles) and measure end-to-end packet loss rate. Verify that measured packet loss does not exceed the customer-defined threshold (e.g., ≤1%). Log packet loss metrics and compare against acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit teleoperation control commands from the tele-operator interface to the remote equipment with fidelity sufficient to preserve operator intent.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit telemetry and sensor data from the remote equipment to the tele-operator interface with fidelity sufficient to support situational awareness.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The NGHE solution shall detect and report communication degradation (packet loss, latency, jitter) to the operator and system in real-time.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time network quality metrics (loss rate, latency variance) to maintain command/feedback integrity within customer-defined thresholds.
DP2: Establish a dual-channel communication architecture with independent network paths and automatic failover logic to ensure continuous teleoperation control availability.
DP3: Deploy application-level forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy levels, tuned to customer network conditions, to recover lost packets without retransmission.
DP4: Implement a quality-of-service (QoS) negotiation and traffic prioritization layer that reserves bandwidth for critical control signals and adapts payload compression dynamically.

DP5: Use a hybrid approach combining lightweight heartbeat monitoring, selective acknowledgment (SACK) of critical frames, and adaptive timeout-driven retransmission to balance latency and reliability.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Use a hybrid approach combining lightweight heartbeat monitoring, selective acknowledgment (SACK) of critical frames, and adaptive timeout-driven retransmission to balance latency and reliability.

potential_sources: Heartbeat monitoring is loosely coupled to retransmission logic via shared network state (loss rate estimation)—acceptable if state is read-only to heartbeat module.
SACK mechanism depends on frame classification (critical vs. non-critical), which must be defined consistently across command/feedback layers—requires clear interface contract.
Adaptive timeout tuning couples to network condition sensing; mitigation: encapsulate timeout calculation in dedicated utility module with well-defined input/output.
DP5 assumes bidirectional communication channel; if unidirectional constraints exist, coupling increases significantly—must be validated against actual network topology.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 avoids hard technology lock-in (no specific protocol mandated) and supports customer-defined thresholds via configurable parameters. Coupling is manageable through clear module boundaries (heartbeat, SACK classifier, timeout calculator, retransmission engine) and explicit state-sharing contracts. Preferred over DP1 (requires continuous metric polling, higher overhead) and DP2 (dual-channel cost/complexity may exceed customer constraints). DP3 and DP4 are valid but DP5 offers better latency characteristics for teleoperation (lower added delay than FEC, more responsive than QoS reservation alone).

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer network conditions are stable or change slowly enough for adaptive algorithms to converge (timescale > 10s of seconds). RISK: Rapid network degradation (e.g., sudden congestion) may cause transient loss spikes exceeding 1% before adaptation. MITIGATION: Define acceptable transient overshoot and test with synthetic network stress profiles.

ASSUMPTION: Bidirectional communication is available (operator → robot and robot → operator feedback). RISK: If network is unidirectional or has asymmetric loss, SACK and retransmission logic must be redesigned. MITIGATION: Validate network topology early; if asymmetric, consider DP3 (FEC) as fallback.

ASSUMPTION: Packet loss is random (Poisson-like) rather than bursty. RISK: Burst loss (e.g., 10 consecutive packets lost) may exceed SACK recovery window, causing command gaps. MITIGATION: Implement burst detection and increase SACK window size or trigger FEC fallback under burst conditions.

ASSUMPTION: Operator latency tolerance is ≥ 100–200 ms (typical for teleoperation). RISK: Aggressive retransmission and adaptive timeouts may add 50–150 ms; if customer requires <100 ms, DP5 may not meet latency SLA. MITIGATION: Measure end-to-end latency budget early; if tight, consider DP2 (dual-channel) or hybrid DP5+DP3.

ASSUMPTION: Customer can define and communicate 'agreed network conditions' (e.g., 'LTE with 5% baseline loss, <50 ms latency'). RISK: Vague or changing network assumptions invalidate acceptance criteria. MITIGATION: Establish formal network SLA document with customer; include test scenarios and measurement methodology.

ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation commands are small (< 1 KB per frame) and can tolerate minor reordering. RISK: Large or order-sensitive payloads (e.g., video streams, complex sensor arrays) may require additional sequencing logic. MITIGATION: Separate control plane (DP5) from data plane; apply DP5 only to critical control, use DP3 or DP4 for bulk data.

RISK: Heartbeat overhead and SACK signaling add network traffic; under high loss, feedback traffic itself may be lost, creating cascading failures. MITIGATION: Implement exponential backoff for heartbeat and limit SACK frequency; test under worst-case loss scenarios.

RISK: Timeout-driven retransmission introduces jitter; if operator expects deterministic latency, variance may degrade usability. MITIGATION: Measure and publish latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99); set customer expectations accordingly.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity by ensuring that critical control commands and feedback frames are delivered with ≤ 1% packet loss (or customer-defined target) under agreed network conditions, while maintaining operator latency within [customer-specified range, e.g., 100–300 ms]. The solution shall support adaptive recovery mechanisms (retransmission, FEC, or failover) tuned to customer network profiles (e.g., LTE, WiFi, satellite) and shall gracefully degrade non-critical data (e.g., auxiliary telemetry) under congestion. Acceptance criteria: (1) Packet loss of critical frames ≤ 1% in baseline network scenario; (2) End-to-end latency p95 ≤ [customer threshold]; (3) Configurable network profiles and thresholds; (4) Documented failure modes and recovery times under burst loss or network partition. DP5 is selected as the primary design approach, with DP3 (FEC) reserved as a fallback for burst-loss scenarios or asymmetric networks. Implementation shall separate control plane (DP5) from data plane (DP3/DP4) to avoid coupling and allow independent tuning.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all remote control communication protocols, including heartbeat monitoring, selective acknowledgment of critical frames, adaptive timeout-driven retransmission, and latency-reliability trade-off logic for teleoperated vehicle commands and feedback.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Transmits heartbeat packets, SACK frames, and retransmitted critical commands; receives acknowledgments and network status feedback.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and control inputs that require reliable delivery with adaptive timeout tuning.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports vehicle state and communication health metrics (latency, packet loss, retransmission counts) for monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals critical communication failures (e.g., heartbeat timeout, loss of SACK on safety-critical frames) to Safety Services.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: heartbeat, SACK, and adaptive retransmission are core teleoperation protocol concerns. Latency-reliability trade-off is inherent to remote control. Minor diffusion: timeout parameters may need tuning from Safety Services; network-level metrics flow to Telemetry.

Poor fit: Safety Services should consume safety alerts, not own the communication protocol. Heartbeat and SACK logic are not safety-specific; they are communication-layer concerns. High diffusion: protocol state, retransmission logic, and timeout adaptation would leak into multiple modules.

Weak fit: Vehicle module typically owns actuators and sensors, not remote communication protocols. Heartbeat and SACK are teleoperation-specific, not vehicle-specific. High diffusion: protocol logic would be misplaced; vehicle would need to expose internal communication state.

Weak fit: NGHE Solution is likely a top-level system container. Placing protocol-level DP here violates separation of concerns and makes the boundary too broad. High diffusion: protocol details would scatter across subsystems; no clear ownership.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP5 because heartbeat monitoring, selective acknowledgment, and adaptive timeout-driven retransmission are fundamental to reliable remote control communication. These mechanisms directly address the latency-reliability trade-off inherent in teleoperated systems. The module's responsibility is to ensure that operator commands reach the vehicle with appropriate reliability guarantees and that feedback is timely. All protocol state, timeout adaptation logic, and SACK decision-making remain cohesive within this boundary.
containment_risks: Timeout parameters may need to be tuned dynamically based on safety criticality; this requires a clean interface to Safety Services (mitigated by SafetyAlert output interface).
Network-level metrics (packet loss, latency) must be observable for diagnostics; this is addressed by Telemetry output interface.

If vehicle-specific communication constraints emerge (e.g., bandwidth limits, protocol variants), the DP may need refinement to separate vehicle-agnostic protocol logic from vehicle-specific adaptation (recommend DP revision if this occurs).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% or customer-defined target under agreed network conditions). The Teleoperation IM boundary owns heartbeat monitoring, SACK, and adaptive retransmission—all directly observable and testable. Telemetry interface exports packet loss and retransmission metrics, enabling direct verification against the acceptance criterion. SafetyAlert interface provides a clear signal path for critical communication failures, supporting safety-case validation.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The FR requirement maps unambiguously to the Teleoperation module. The IM boundary is well-scoped: it encapsulates all mechanisms needed to maintain communication integrity (heartbeat, SACK, adaptive timeout logic) and exports the observables needed to verify compliance (latency, packet loss, retransmission counts via Telemetry). Ownership is clear and non-ambiguous.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP directly addresses the FR. Hybrid heartbeat + SACK + adaptive timeout-driven retransmission is a sound technical approach to maintaining communication integrity under packet loss. The DP explicitly balances latency and reliability, which is the core trade-off in teleoperation. The mechanism is customer-configurable (timeout parameters, SACK thresholds) and measurable against the acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1%). No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Timeout parameter tuning: Adaptive timeout logic may require dynamic adjustment based on safety criticality signals from Safety Services. Mitigation: SafetyAlert output interface provides a clean, one-way signal path; Safety Services should not own timeout logic, only influence it via well-defined alerts. Recommend documenting the timeout adaptation algorithm and its sensitivity to safety signals.

Network-level metrics observability: Packet loss, latency, and retransmission counts must flow to Telemetry for diagnostics and compliance verification. Mitigation: Telemetry output interface is already defined. Ensure metrics are sampled and exported at sufficient granularity for root-cause analysis.

Vehicle-specific protocol variants: If future vehicle integrations require protocol adaptations (e.g., bandwidth constraints, proprietary acknowledgment schemes), the DP may need decomposition to separate vehicle-agnostic heartbeat/SACK logic from vehicle-specific adaptation. Current risk is low if vehicles conform to a standard teleoperation interface; escalate if heterogeneous vehicle support is required.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). The Teleoperation module is the natural, cohesive owner of DP5. Heartbeat monitoring, SACK, and adaptive retransmission are fundamental teleoperation protocol concerns, not scattered across Safety Services, Vehicle, or top-level system boundaries. All protocol state and decision logic remain within the module. External interfaces (Network Link, Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) are clean and minimal, with no evidence of DP logic leaking into other modules. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable and well-mitigated by interface design.

recommended_revisions: Document the adaptive timeout algorithm explicitly: specify how timeout values are initialized, updated in response to observed packet loss, and adjusted based on safety criticality signals from SafetyAlert. Include bounds and convergence guarantees.

Define SACK decision criteria: clarify which frames are considered 'critical' (e.g., safety-critical commands, emergency stop) and which are eligible for selective acknowledgment. Provide a configuration mechanism for customers to adjust criticality thresholds.

Specify Telemetry export granularity: define the sampling interval, aggregation method, and precision for packet loss, latency, and retransmission metrics. Ensure diagnostics can distinguish between transient packet loss and sustained degradation.

Add a DP refinement trigger: if vehicle-specific protocol constraints emerge (e.g., bandwidth limits, proprietary handshakes), decompose DP5 into a vehicle-agnostic core (heartbeat + SACK + timeout) and a vehicle-specific adaptation layer. Document the decomposition strategy in advance.

Validate against customer network conditions: ensure the DP is tested under the agreed network conditions (latency range, packet loss distribution, jitter profile) before deployment. Include acceptance test cases that verify packet loss ≤ 1% under worst-case conditions.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR5 is well-formed, DP is sound, and IM containment is excellent (0.92). However, the recommended revisions and diffusion risks—particularly timeout parameter tuning, SACK criticality decision logic, and vehicle-specific protocol variants—indicate that the current DP level is too coarse for safe, configurable implementation. High safety criticality demands explicit decomposition of adaptive timeout behavior, safety-signal coupling, and protocol variant handling into sub-FRs. This ensures that timeout adaptation, SACK decision criteria, and vehicle-specific constraints are each owned by a clear sub-module with measurable acceptance criteria, rather than remaining implicit in the monolithic DP5. Decomposition also enables independent verification of each concern and clearer traceability to safety requirements.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Maintain adaptive timeout values that respond to observed packet loss and safety criticality signals, ensuring convergence to stable retransmission intervals within 5 seconds of network condition change, with bounds [T_min, T_max] configurable per customer and safety domain.

FR5.2: Classify outbound frames as safety-critical or non-critical based on command type and safety context, and apply selective acknowledgment (SACK) only to non-critical frames, ensuring all safety-critical commands receive full acknowledgment before execution.

FR5.3: Export packet loss, latency, and retransmission metrics to Telemetry at configurable sampling intervals (default 1 Hz), with sufficient precision to distinguish transient loss (<1 second) from sustained degradation (>10 seconds), enabling root-cause diagnostics and compliance verification.

FR5.4: Detect and adapt to vehicle-specific protocol constraints (bandwidth limits, proprietary acknowledgment schemes, latency bounds) by applying a vehicle-agnostic heartbeat/SACK/timeout core and a pluggable vehicle-specific adaptation layer, with clear interface contracts and fallback to standard teleoperation protocol if adaptation fails.

revise.why: DP5 is logically sound but operationally underspecified. The recommended revisions require explicit sub-DP definitions for: (1) adaptive timeout algorithm with safety-signal coupling, (2) SACK criticality classification logic, (3) Telemetry metric export strategy, and (4) vehicle-specific protocol adaptation. These sub-DPs should be derived from the sub-FRs above and documented with algorithm pseudocode, configuration schemas, and test cases. Current DP5 should be elevated to a DP summary that references the sub-DPs, ensuring traceability and enabling independent verification of each concern.

assumptions: Timeout adaptation algorithm converges within 5 seconds of network condition change (assumption to be validated in FR5.1 sub-decomposition).
Safety-critical frame classification is deterministic and can be derived from command type and safety context (assumption to be validated in FR5.2 sub-decomposition).

Vehicle-specific protocol constraints can be abstracted into a pluggable adaptation layer without compromising core heartbeat/SACK/timeout logic (assumption to be validated in FR5.4 sub-decomposition).
Telemetry sampling at 1 Hz is sufficient for diagnostics and compliance verification (assumption to be validated in FR5.3 sub-decomposition).
SafetyAlert interface provides a clean, one-way signal path for safety criticality updates without creating circular dependencies (assumption to be validated during IM design).
open_risks: Timeout parameter tuning sensitivity: Adaptive timeout logic may oscillate or diverge if safety-signal coupling is not carefully designed. Mitigation: FR5.1 sub-decomposition must include convergence analysis and bounds validation.

SACK criticality misclassification: If frame criticality classification is incorrect or incomplete, safety-critical commands may be selectively acknowledged, violating safety requirements. Mitigation: FR5.2 sub-decomposition must include exhaustive classification rules and test coverage for all command types.

Vehicle-specific protocol variants: If vehicle integrations require heterogeneous protocol adaptations, the pluggable adaptation layer may become complex and difficult to maintain. Mitigation: FR5.4 sub-decomposition must define clear interface contracts and version management strategy.

Telemetry metric precision loss: If metrics are aggregated or sampled at insufficient granularity, root-cause diagnostics may be inconclusive. Mitigation: FR5.3 sub-decomposition must specify sampling interval, aggregation method, and precision requirements.

Safety-signal latency: If SafetyAlert signals are delayed or lost, timeout adaptation may not respond promptly to safety criticality changes. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface design must include latency bounds and loss detection mechanisms (to be validated during IM design).
evidence_needed: Adaptive timeout algorithm specification: pseudocode, convergence proof, sensitivity analysis to safety-signal timing and magnitude.
SACK criticality classification rules: exhaustive mapping of command types to criticality levels, with rationale and test cases.
Telemetry metric export specification: sampling interval, aggregation method, precision, and diagnostic use cases.
Vehicle-specific protocol adaptation strategy: interface contracts, version management, fallback behavior, and test cases for heterogeneous vehicle support.
Safety-signal coupling design: latency bounds, loss detection, and impact on timeout adaptation behavior.
Acceptance test cases: verification of packet loss ≤ 1% under agreed network conditions, timeout convergence within 5 seconds, SACK criticality correctness, and Telemetry metric accuracy.
Customer configuration schema: timeout bounds [T_min, T_max], SACK thresholds, Telemetry sampling interval, and vehicle-specific adaptation parameters.
